This allows them to improve therapy selection for patients, disease characterization, and biopharma drug development. Essentially, CAI has five main pillars in its platform. First, the 1) MI Profile is a tissue-based molecular profiling platform that analyzes tumor tissues and studies their genomic and molecular features. Secondly, 2) Caris Assure is a testing modality based on blood samples. This platform analyzes liquid biopsy rather than tissue samples. CAI offers its MI Profile and Caris Assure to clients as diagnostics solutions. They recently launched the Caris ChromoSeq for detecting blood cancers and related hematologic disorders using bone marrow aspirate or peripheral blood samples.

Moreover, 3) CAI's AI and dataset tool has multi-modal clinico-genomic information that can be used with AI and ML to improve insights, biomarker discovery, and product development. The company states that its dataset covers over 23,000 genes, with more than 1 million cases profiled. CAI's current commercial programs execute whole exome sequencing (WES) that reads only the protein-coding parts, the exomes, which usually are the sections that contain disease-causing mutations. The company states that it has analyzed around 627,000 whole-exome cases. It also provides whole transcriptome sequencing (WTS) and whole genome sequencing (WGS).

Lastly, CAI's platform is, at its core, a collaborative network of cancer centers, researchers, and clinicians. As a whole, these entities form a 4) Precision Oncology Alliance (POA) with over 99 members. The purpose of CAI's POA is to help with R&D, data collection, and even facilitate clinical trials for new drug/therapy candidates. In that sense, CAI's fifth pillar is its 5) Drug Discovery and Data Analytics platform. Here, CAI connects with biopharma companies to support the development of new oncology candidates.

Caris' Pipeline Solutions

Looking ahead, CAI's pipeline is working on Caris Detect, which is a multi-cancer early detection (MCED) blood test. MCED looks for biological signals in a blood sample that suggest a person may already have cancer, ideally before the disease has been formally diagnosed. CAI says that this new MCED test will use WGS and machine learning to detect those early signals. CAI is currently progressing here with a clinical validation study called ACHIEVE 1. This trial has already reported an interim readout that suggested meaningful cancer detection even in stage 1 and stage 2, with high specificity (i.e., this test yields low false positives).

Aside from the Caris Detect, CAI's pipeline has the Caris MI Clarity. This is a breast cancer recurrence-risk test for patients who are estrogen receptor-positive (ER-positive), human epidermal growth factor receptor 2-negative (HER2-negative), and are in Stage I or II. The main benefit of this test is that the added HypoG-01 context helps assess late-recurrence risks much better for women. This demographic otherwise typically shows average pathology, so the Caris MI Clarity provides them with a clearer risk assessment at a molecular level.

Similarly, CAI is working on the Caris MRD Tumor Naïve testing solution. This is a minimal residual disease (MRD) tool for colorectal cancer (CRC). And, in the same vein, the Caris MRD Tumor Informed is another MRD program that uses WGS. At their core, all of CAI's testing offerings help patients identify trackers and then monitor for residual disease markers, which is vital for managing and treating cancer in general.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, CAI currently trades at a $5.1 billion market cap. Its latest 10-K report shows its balance sheet holds $797.8 million in cash and $2.3 million in short-term investments. This amounts to roughly $800.1 million in liquid resources against $379.0 million in debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). Based on those figures, I estimate CAI's EV at approximately $4.7 billion.

Moreover, the analysts' consensus projects CAI will generate $1.2 billion in revenues by 2027, which would price the stock at a forward EV/S of 3.9. For comparison, its sector's median forward EV/S is 3.2. So, in that sense, CAI would appear to trade at a slight premium already. However, I do think a premium is justified, as CAI's YoY top-line growth of 97.0% substantially outpaces its peers' median sales growth of just 7.4%. In fact, CAI is essentially growing 13.1x faster than its peers. If anything, I would argue the stock still has room to run in the long-term due to this.

Additionally, I calculate that CAI became cash flow positive in 2025 to the tune of $66.9 million. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its 2025 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. And, while this may seem low relative to its market cap, it's a huge improvement relative to 2024. Using the same calculation method, I estimate CAI actually burned through $253.6 million during 2024. In other words, CAI had a positive $320.5 million cash flow swing in a single year, showcasing that the company has hit a major inflection point.

Naturally, the stock is now down 54.0% from its 2025 highs. I think the negative sentiment is likely due to the initial IPO hype wearing off to some extent. But also, note that CAI's 2025 full-year revenues grew 125% YoY, mostly due to a 150% increase in ASP. Sales volumes actually grew only 20% YoY by comparison. So, maybe the market is also somewhat skeptical about massive revenue jumps going forward, as I would imagine further similar increases in ASP are unlikely. And, on the other hand, CAI is guiding to a 19%-20% increase in operating expenses to fund its revenue growth into 2026.

Having said that, CAI was able to hike up its ASP because the FDA approved its new MI Cancer Seek. This is an improved tissue assay component of CAI's MI Profile solution. MI Cancer Seek also received Medicare coverage under the National Coverage Determination (NCD) for next-generation sequencing. And the Medicare fee-for-service pricing increased from $3,500 to $8,455.

Generally speaking, management mentioned that the FDA's approval improved their position with certain payer types and helped them secure better deals. In other words, CAI benefited from regulatory approvals and more favorable coverage policies. So, this clearly highlights how dependent CAI's business is on favorable regulations and insurance policies, which is a risk worth considering. But other than that, I think this type of risk is par for the course for all investors in this sector.

Conclusion: Long-Term Bullish

Overall, CAI has an undoubtedly successful formula for oncology and diagnostics. Shortly after the IPO, the stock seems to have gotten ahead of itself. And indeed, I think CAI's prospects still have some uncertainties regarding its future margins as it scales up its sales volumes without substantially hiking up its ASP any further. As such, I think it's reasonable to have a more sober bullish stance to account for those factors. However, after such a steep pullback post-IPO, I believe the stock is now trading at a much more reasonable valuation for long-term investors. That's why I ultimately rate the stock a viable "Buy" at these levels.

data.title: 3M: The PFAS Cloud Is Lifting, And What's Underneath Looks Really Good
data.description:
3M Company (MMM) has rebounded more than 50% from its 2023 lows, but it's still nowhere near its 2021 or 2018 levels. MMM has been plagued with litigation, from the PFAS lawsuits to the earplug settlements. Then adding in the Solventum (SOLV) spinoff, which caused some investors to question what type of company MMM was anymore, caused MMM to remain an underperformer. Every time it looked like the bottom was in, another headline would drag the stock lower and remind everyone why many investors had moved on. I am taking a contrarian approach, as I believe something is shifting and not enough people are paying attention to it. MMM just capped off a full year where adjusted operating margins expanded by 200 basis points to 23.4% and their EPS grew 10% to $8.06. Yes, PFAS is still a real thing, and there are roughly 15,000 personal injury lawsuits still pending and bellwether trials expected later this year, but the largest liabilities are resolved, structured, and being paid out over 13 years. The market is pricing in high single-digit forward growth on EPS over the next several years, and the average analyst price target is $173.67. At a time when SaaS is imploding and the A.I. revolution is being punished due to CapEx allocations, MMM is starting to look more enticing as the litigations are being put behind them.

Following up on my previous article about MMM

Back in May of 2025, I had written an article about MMM (can be read here), and even though MMM had rebounded by over 40% off the lows, I was still bullish. Management was focused on innovation and operational efficiency after a strong Q1, and I believed that the litigation risks overcompensated the progress MMM had made. MMM's strategy revolved around accelerating product innovation, improving margins, and disciplined capital allocation by rewarding shareholders through buybacks and dividends. I am following up with a new article to discuss how the 2025 fiscal year went, what I am excited about for 2026, and why I believe there is still value to be unlocked for MMM. In an age where technology dominates the headlines, companies such as MMM don't get discussed often, and sometimes there are opportunities where others aren't looking.

The litigation breakdown, including PFAS

This article wouldn't be worth reading if I didn't address the litigation troubles, including PFAS, for all the readers. PFAS is per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, the forever chemicals that have been the single biggest drag on this MMM's multiple for the better part of 3 years. The lawsuits were massive, the uncertainty was paralyzing, and the market priced MMM like it might not survive the financial burden. In the 10-K they outlined many of these aspects, and CEO William Brown addressed litigation concerns on the Q4 earnings call. After going through everything, I am less concerned than before, and even though there are still risks, I don't believe they are nearly as elevated as they once were.

Based on my research, here is where things stand for MMM. The public water system settlement received final court approval back in April 2024. Under the agreement, MMM is paying between $10.5 billion and $12.5 billion over 13 years to public water suppliers across the country. The first phase will start with payments to systems that had already detected PFAS above certain thresholds. Phase two will cover systems that detected PFAS more recently or are still testing, as there is a claim deadline of July 31, 2026. This is an opt-out class settlement, which means that public water systems that didn't explicitly opt out are covered and have released MMM from all past and future liability related to PFAS in their drinking water. The fact that MMM is released from future litigation allows them to close the door on this without future financial implications.

MMM still has the personal injury side of the litigation to deal with. There are approximately 15,000 individual lawsuits pending in the federal MDL alleging cancer and other health issues tied to PFAS exposure. Bellwether trials are expected to begin later in 2026, which is a legitimate risk. I believe that the existential financial threat to MMM was always in the public water system claims because of their scale. With those resolved, I believe that the personal injury cases will likely be negotiated or settled over time the way most mass tort cases play out. Keep in mind I am not a lawyer, and I am making that statement as an opinion from reading through the information from the court filings, MMM commentary, and earnings reports.

Q4 results and the 2025 fiscal year are telling a different story for MMM

In Q4 2025, MMM reported that its revenue increased 2.1% YoY to $6 billion, while its adjusted EPS came in at $1.83, which was an increase of 9% YoY and ahead of the consensus estimates. Their adjusted operating margin exceeded 20% as it expanded 140 basis points YoY. I think that it's important that the GAAP numbers look slim because of litigation charges, and that could create a disconnect in the market. While GAAP EPS was $1.07, down 20% YoY because of a $0.56 per share charge for litigation costs, on an adjusted basis, which strips out those one-time legal charges, the non-GAAP EPS was $0.66 higher and a beat by $0.03 from where analysts had it coming in. On an annualized basis, MMM's adjusted operating margin reached 23.4%, which expanded 200 basis points, and the full-year adjusted EPS was $8.06, up 10% YoY. Organic sales growth for the year was 2.1%, with growth accelerating from 1.5% in the first half to 2.7% in the second half. MMM was able to return $4.8 billion to shareholders through a combination of $3.25 billion in buybacks and $1.55 billion in dividends paid out.

When I look at the individual sectors of MMM, the investment thesis is looking more compelling. Safety & Industrial is their largest segment, generating 46% of the annualized revenue, and in Q4 it delivered 3.8% of organic sales growth YoY. When I look at the 2025 fiscal year, growth accelerated from 2.5% in the first half to 3.9% in the second half. Their transportation & electronics segment, which makes up 34% of the revenue, posted organic sales growth of 2.4% in Q4 and an acceleration of growth from 1% to 3% in the second half of the year compared to the first half of the year. Within this segment, MMM has been building out their aerospace division, with sales doubling over the past 4 years. The weak spot was the consumer segment, which represents roughly 20% of the revenue and had declined 2.2% YoY. This was due to weaker consumer sentiment and sluggish retail traffic. Consumer weakness and aspects of the macroeconomic environment tightening impact the consumer segment, but in the context of the broader business, the segments that matter most are performing well with forward growth.

I'm bullish for 2026 because management guided for 3% in adjusted organic sales growth and for their operating margin to expand by roughly 70-80 basis points. This would expand MMM's margins to around 25% and allow MMM to generate between $8.50 and $8.70. If MMM can get to a 25% margin and sustain it, we're talking about a fundamentally different earnings power than what the market has been pricing in during the litigation years. MMM has already delivered 200+ basis points of expansion in each of the last 2 years, so believing that management is going to deliver under 100 basis points of expansion in 2026 isn't out of the question. In my opinion, the growth is clear, as MMM's specialty films and adhesives are critical for the AI data center buildout in the semiconductor industry. MMM just announced a capacity investment to more than double EBO production to meet AI data center demand. I think people are writing MMM due to the litigations without realizing that there is $2.5 billion in planned buybacks for 2026 on top of the dividend in addition to margin and EPS growth.

Bill Brown is running a different MMM than prior leadership

When Bill Brown took over as CEO in 2024, there is no question that he inherited a mess. The innovation pipeline had dried up as they used to launch over 1,000 new products a year until the litigation era. In 2025, MMM launched twice as many new products as it did in 2023, and management is targeting 350 new product launches in 2026 with a goal of reaching 1,000 again by 2027. They're committing $3.5 billion to R&D over a 3-year period that ends in 2027. Currently about 10% of sales come from products introduced in the last 5 years, and the goal for MMM is to increase that to 20% by 2027. Mr. Brown's strategy revolves around getting products from R&D to market faster and cutting through the corporate bureaucracy that had bogged MMM down in prior eras. He is prioritizing high-margin and growth products over legacy low-margin lines. He is also looking to divest roughly 10% of its 120 profit centers over the next few years to focus on bigger opportunities.

I think that the SOLV spinoff in April 2024 was a critical piece of this vision. By separating the healthcare business into its own company, MMM could focus MMM purely on its materials science and industrial roots. MMM still holds a portion of SOLV equity that it can monetize to fund legal payments or further deleverage. Several weeks ago, MMM partnered with Bain Capital to acquire Madison Fire & Rescue for $1.95 billion with the intention of combining it with MMM's Scott Safety brand. I think that this is a capital-efficient way to expand in fire and safety that can help surpass margin expansion goals. Overall, Mr. Brown is looking towards the future and to take MMM in a different direction, and the market isn't taking this into consideration, considering MMM moves past the litigation phase and gets back to offense as it can now focus on the business.

MMM looks interesting from a valuation and capital allocation perspective

When I look at MMM's earnings projections compared to Honeywell (HON), it makes me believe that there is value to be unlocked. The analyst consensus numbers for MMM indicate that MMM will produce $8.66 of EPS in 2026, $9.40 in 2027, and then $10.02 in 2028. MMM is trading at 17.4 times 2026 earnings, with 17.70% of EPS growth expected over the next 2 years. MMM is trading at 14.8 times 2028 earnings, which puts it in deep value territory in my opinion. HON trades at 18.6 times 2028 earnings with slightly more EPS growth expected (20%), which makes me believe that MMM is trading at a discount.

MMM rightsized the dividend after the SOLV spinoff, and since then it's grown the dividend for the past 2 years. The quarterly dividend has increased by 4.29% ($0.03) in 2025 and 6.85% ($0.05) at the beginning of 2026. MMM is still a dividend growth company paying $3.12 per share, which is a 2.08% yield. Investors are getting paid roughly 1/3rd of the EPS in the form of the dividend while MMM is buying back billions of dollars worth of shares. MMM is looking to create shareholder value by increasing revenue and profitability while staying true to their capital allocation plan.

What I am looking for in MMM's upcoming earnings

MMM is reporting Q1 2026 earnings on 4/21, and it will be a big test for management. The Street is looking for $1.97 of GAAP EPS and revenue to come in at $6.01 billion. In Q1 2025, MMM delivered adjusted EPS of $1.88 on adjusted sales of $5.8 billion with 1.5% organic growth and a 23.5% adjusted operating margin. Q1 2025 was actually the slowest organic growth quarter of the year at 1.5%, as growth accelerated to 2.5% in Q2, 3.2% in Q3, and 2.2% in Q4. Management guided for approximately 3% organic growth for all of 2026, and they specifically said they expect growth to accelerate across all 3 business groups this year. I am watching to see if the market believes that what MMM produces in Q1 is a validation of their guidance, as that would help put the litigation risks behind them to a degree. I also want to see margins expand, considering the guidance is that there will be roughly 70-80 basis points of expansion. I am also looking for an indication that MMM is getting back to their roots with new product launches. If there is significant progress here, it is because the innovation narrative is critical to the bull case for MMM.

The risks to investing in MMM

While I think there is value to be unlocked with MMM, there are several risk factors to consider. MMM is transitioning from a litigation story to an execution story, but the personal injury PFAS trials are a wildcard. If bellwether verdicts come in significantly worse than expected, the market is likely to punish this stock regardless of operating performance. We're seeing consumer softness that could persist or deepen if the U.S. economy weakens further. There are also unknown tariff risks, as escalation between the U.S. and trading partners could hit 3M's international business. There's also execution risk with the turnaround itself, as there is no guarantee that the results from 2025 will continue to be replicated. Investors should do their own due diligence because there are significant risks to consider with MMM.

Conclusion

The largest PFAS financial liabilities are resolved and structured, which leads me to believe that MMM can transition to an execution story and shed the litigation image as their financial metrics improve. Mr. Brown is doing exactly what you'd want a turnaround CEO to do, as the focus is on accelerating innovation, expanding margins, cutting bureaucracy, and returning capital to shareholders. MMM is trading at less than 15 times forward earnings, is buying back shares, and is growing the dividend. I think that after a few more quarters of solid results, the market will reprice MMM to the upside, especially if there is positive movement on the bellwether verdicts.

data.title: D.R. Horton Vs. Lennar: Homebuilder Showdown As Mortgage Rates Jump
data.description:
The possibility of achieving the American dream of owning a home moves up and down to the beat of mortgage rates, which are highly sensitive to interest rates, which are highly sensitive to inflation.

That circle of life is the backdrop to a homebuilder sector that experienced a 10% drawdown since the start of the Iran war, led by its two biggest proponents, the two largest homebuilders in the US, D.R. Horton (DHI) and Lennar (LEN) (LEN.B).

While both are set to struggle in the face of higher mortgage rates and stubborn inflation, I argue D.R. Horton is better positioned to withstand the storm and is the more attractive long-term play.

I previously argued that D.R. Horton is the more attractive homebuilder, and I maintain that view.

Iran War And Its Effect On The Homebuilder Circle Of Life

The Iran war has a lot of implications for the market, of course. But, in the homebuilders' case, the story is fairly narrow and simple.

There are plenty of Americans who would love to buy a home. In homebuilding, it's less about whether a customer wants your product; it's much more about whether they can afford it.

In February 2026, we've reached the highest level of housing affordability since the pandemic, with Mortgage Rates being one of the key metrics to push the index up (lower rates = higher affordability).

There is that "magic" line of 6%, where mortgage rates are considered low enough to allow affordability and ignite the market. With the start of the Iran war, we've bounced from that magic line back to 6.4%, the highest level since September 2025.

That's the result of rising inflation, which was depicted in the March CPI print, with the primary source of inflation being energy, amid continued blockage in the Strait of Hormuz.

In Homebuilder Investing - You Have To Be A Market Timer Or A True Long-Term Investor

I laid out my comprehensive Homebuilder Investor 101 guide in an August 2024 article covering D.R. Horton. Before we get to choosing the right play, it's worth briefly going over the key drivers of stock performance in the sector, which will be the criteria to judge whether Horton or Lennar is the right choice.

In essence, the homebuilder business model used to be an asset-heavy business, where builders bought land, developed it, built homes, and then sold them. The cash cycle in this type of business is quite terrible, as it takes, optimistically, 5+ years to complete one such cycle, and it's not like margins are that great at the end (gross margins of 20%-25% are the standard).

Meanwhile, you're exposed to affordability cycles, input cost inflation, and have to spend a good amount of money on developing the land.

About a decade ago, D.R. Horton realized that what they should do is outsource the land development aspect and, instead, build a meaningful option portfolio. It's been off to the races ever since:

During this period, homebuilders that made the transition successfully were able to keep up, while those who didn't stayed behind, as reflected by the gap between DHI and Lennar.

Today, homebuilders are judged by how asset-light they are, how efficient they are at building, and what idiosyncratic risks they carry (geographic concentration, bad balance sheet, poor management, etc.).

This criterion also tells you which of them are better positioned to withstand down cycles of higher inflation.

No Matter Which Metric You Choose, D.R. Horton Beats Lennar

D.R. Horton, being the largest homebuilder in America, also has the industry's largest lot position, with 590,500 lots. However, only 25% of those lots are owned (the old model), while 75% are optioned (the new model).

Meanwhile, Lennar is not shy of lots either. The second-largest builder in America has, well, the second-largest lot position, and while they say 98% of it isn't fully owned, the story is more complicated, as they've only now completed the spinoff that drove that change.

For the sake of simplicity, the easy way to measure how "asset light" Lennar is vs. D.R. Horton is simply by looking at their respective returns, where DHI wins by a mile:

Similarly, Horton is also the far more efficient homebuilder. While homebuilding is a game of scale, the scale difference between the two isn't that big, so it's more reflective of management quality and business model progress.

Lastly, both companies are large in scale and are quite diversified across geographies and price points. They are also far from being leveraged at concerning levels.

So, I'd say it's a very clear win for Horton based on fundamentals. What about valuation?

Valuation

Here's the shocking part. At current valuation levels, Lennar actually trades at a premium over D.R. Horton, trading at 15 times forward earnings vs. 14 times for Horton.

On a forward 1Y basis, they are at similar levels, reflecting the expectation that Lennar will grow earnings faster as it starts to capitalize on its business transformation.

Meanwhile, on a price-to-book basis, D.R. Horton trades at a much higher valuation, but that's a metric that is becoming less relevant under the new business model.

All in all, I think it's a lopsided win for D.R. Horton on fundamentals, and a tie on valuation, resulting in a clear preference for Horton. I strongly believe that if and when we get to the up-cycle, Horton will outperform Lennar. In addition, I find the risk-reward much more attractive with DHI ahead of a continuation of the down cycle in light of rising inflation.

What About Avoiding Homebuilders Altogether?

That we concluded D.R. Horton is the better play doesn't mean it's necessarily an attractive addition to investor portfolios. The way I see it, investing in homebuilders isn't for everyone. In fact, I'd say most investors should refrain from investing in them.

These are highly cyclical companies that are extremely affected by macro and sentiment. They barely have any moat worth mentioning, and the main competitive advantages come down to scale and management quality.

That said, investors who are looking for near-term, high-risk, high-reward opportunities could very well find them in homebuilders, especially at trough sentiment. In addition, true long-term investors who don't care much about near-term volatility could also do well by allocating some portion of their portfolios to an industry that has very little overlap with the rest of the market and enjoys very strong structural demand.

Conclusion

Homebuilders have sold off as mortgage rates have risen in the face of the Iran war, which is sending inflation expectations higher, pushing away rate cut hopes, and has already driven an increase in consumer prices, led by energy.

Homebuilders are extremely cyclical stocks, and they are not for everyone, but for investors who want exposure, I find D.R. Horton to be the clear number one pick.

As such, I reiterate Horton at a 'Buy', and Lennar at a 'Hold.'
